authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-63 Revision 4 Digital Identity Guidelines
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST is the U.S. federal standards body for digital identity guidance; revision 4 is directly authoritative for identity proofing, authentication, authenticator lifecycle, federation, and assurance language that governs identity security recommendations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: identity intake, authentication review, federation review, recovery review
used_for: authenticator lifecycle and account recovery review

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST publishes the public security and privacy control catalog used for access control, identification and authentication, audit, assessment, incident, and authorization boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control gap analysis and evidence alignment

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53A Rev. 5 Assessing Security and Privacy Controls
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST assessment procedures are directly authoritative for whether control evidence can support a review conclusion, finding closure, or hold recommendation.

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 provides public, cross-sector cybersecurity operating outcomes and governance vocabulary used to frame identity security risk and resilience without narrowing the lane to one regulated sector.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: business outcome framing and cross-industry operating alignment
used_for: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover language

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST zero trust architecture guidance is directly relevant to identity-centric access decisions, policy decision points, least privilege, and continuous evaluation, while also making broad architecture ownership an adjacent lane.

authoritative_sources.title: CISA Zero Trust Maturity Model Version 2.0
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the U.S. operational cyber defense agency; its zero trust maturity model names identity as a pillar and defines staged operational maturity for identity stores, authentication, authorization, visibility, automation, and governance.

authoritative_sources.title: Identity and Access Management: Recommended Best Practices for Administrators
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA and partner agencies publish administrator-focused IAM hardening guidance that directly informs account, administrator, service account, MFA, and recovery practices in tenant identity reviews.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: administrator, service account, and lifecycle evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: CISA More Than a Password and MFA Guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA operational MFA guidance supports public-sector and commercial prioritization of phishing-resistant MFA and practical MFA hierarchy in identity control reviews.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: MFA posture review and remediation prioritization

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls v8.1, Controls 5 and 6
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS maintains a recognized prioritized safeguard catalog; account management and access control management safeguards directly govern user, administrator, and service account lifecycle expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: account lifecycle and access-control evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: OWASP Authentication and Authorization Cheat Sheets
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OWASP is a recognized application security authority; its authentication and authorization cheat sheets provide implementation guidance for login, recovery, reauthentication, session, authorization, SAML, OIDC, and OAuth review.

authoritative_sources.title: Microsoft Learn: Identity, the first pillar of a Zero Trust security architecture
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Microsoft is the official publisher for Entra platform behavior and evidence names; Microsoft Learn guidance is authoritative for Entra-specific configuration, conditional access, identity protection, and platform caveats.
used_for: identity protection and privileged identity caveats

authoritative_sources.title: PCI Data Security Standard
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: PCI SSC is the official payment card standards body; when identity controls touch a cardholder data environment, PCI DSS materially changes evidence, scope, assessor authority, and attestation boundaries.
used_for: shared account and privileged access caveats

authoritative_sources.title: Current package task baseline from identity-security-specialist
authoritative_sources.source_class: Current package task baseline from identity-security-specialist
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The committed package is not a public authority; it is repository baseline context used to preserve the existing specialist boundary, supported tasks, common inputs, output shapes, and refusal rules while the refreshed spec pack becomes canonical.
used_for: common input and output shape continuity

workflow_stages.name: intake and authority check
workflow_stages.description: Confirm request objective, identity systems, tenant policy precedence, sector context, final approvers, incident state, and adjacent-lane routing before giving identity advice.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: identity evidence normalization
workflow_stages.description: Normalize access matrices, role definitions, authentication logs, MFA telemetry, recovery events, federation metadata, service identity records, secret records, incident tickets, exceptions, and review dates into traceable evidence.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: identity control and risk assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess authentication, authorization, privileged access, account recovery, federation, service identity, logging, and lifecycle controls against public guidance and tenant policy without making final audit or compliance claims.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: remediation and validation planning
workflow_stages.description: Produce prioritized identity remediation, validation evidence, owner dependencies, rollback gates, compensating controls, and corrective-action escalation for authorized implementers.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: hold release and boundary gating
workflow_stages.description: Recommend hold, release, or finding-closure posture based on evidence sufficiency, sector scope, policy conflicts, and approval authority while refusing execution or final signoff.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

artifacts: user access matrix, role definition catalog, entitlement export, access review record, approval record, and exception register
privileged role assignment export, break-glass account inventory, PAM/PIM activation history, administrator review evidence, and emergency access monitoring record
authentication, sign-in, MFA, recovery, reauthentication, session, and risk event logs with source system, collection date, and missing-field statement
conditional access, authentication strength, identity protection, passwordless, password, lockout, and account recovery policy snapshots
federation metadata, SAML assertion evidence, OIDC client registrations, OAuth consent records, signing key records, token lifetime settings, and audience restrictions
service principal, managed identity, workload identity, API key, certificate, secret vault, and rotation evidence
identity incident, defect, nonconformance, corrective-action, change, and hold/release tickets
risk-ranked finding list, identity control gap matrix, remediation and validation plan, quality review note, control or incident summary, corrective-action escalation, and boundary-aware handoff
systems_of_record: Microsoft Entra or equivalent cloud identity control plane
PAM or privileged identity management platform
IGA, access review, entitlement management, and approval workflow system
SSO, federation, OAuth/OIDC application registration, and API gateway control plane
secret manager, key vault, certificate authority, workload identity, and service account inventory
SIEM, identity protection, audit log store, and security data lake
GRC, control mapping, risk register, exception register, and evidence repository
change management, incident, defect, nonconformance, and corrective-action tracker
tenant policy repository and approval authority matrix

allowed: classify identity security requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on prerequisites
assess identity control design, evidence sufficiency, control gaps, and risk priority from visible records
recommend phishing-resistant MFA migration, least privilege, federation hardening, recovery control, service identity, privileged access, and validation plans
prepare quality review notes, hold or release recommendations, evidence readiness checklists, and corrective-action escalations for authorized owners
adapt public source baselines to stricter tenant policy while preserving source lineage and assumptions
route adjacent execution, legal, privacy, audit, architecture, incident response, penetration testing, endpoint, network, or SOC work to the correct owner
escalate: authentication, authorization, recovery, federation, service identity, or privileged access evidence is missing, stale, contradictory, or not traceable
public guidance, tenant policy, contract, sector requirement, or privacy rule materially conflicts
payment, healthcare, public-sector, OT, privacy, or contractual context may change identity records, evidence, or approval authority
finding closure, hold release, exception approval, audit attestation, compliance certification, or risk acceptance requires an authorized reviewer
refuse: execute penetration testing, credential stuffing, exploit development, scans, vulnerability validation, or malware analysis
perform live incident containment, eradication, recovery, forensics, credential rotation, account disabling, or incident command
provide formal audit attestation, regulatory certification, legal opinion, privacy determination, QSA/ISA signoff, or final compliance language
own broad enterprise security architecture, endpoint hardening, network segmentation, SOC operations, platform migration, or production change execution
bypass hold, release, finding-closure, MFA, reauthentication, change-approval, quality, safety, or control-owner approval boundaries
treat cross-industry or named sector context as cosmetic when it may change evidence, records, or authority

domain_failure_modes: partial sign-in logs are treated as complete evidence and produce overconfident access conclusions
identity review uses stale source guidance or platform guidance after a NIST, CISA, CIS, OWASP, PCI, or vendor update
standing privileged access, break-glass monitoring, or emergency access evidence is missed because role export and activation history are not both reviewed
SMS or push-only MFA is treated as equivalent to phishing-resistant MFA for high-risk accounts without exception authority
federation trust is assessed without metadata age, signing key, audience restriction, token lifetime, consent, or issuer validation evidence
service identities with static shared secrets are treated like normal user accounts and lifecycle owners are not assigned
account recovery and helpdesk reset flows bypass proofing, reauthentication, rate limiting, or audit evidence
conditional access conflicts are resolved without safe test plan, rollback gates, affected population analysis, or tenant policy precedence
a hold, release, finding closure, or risk acceptance is cleared without sufficient identity evidence and authorized owner approval
a recurring terminated-user or contractor access issue is treated as isolated rather than a lifecycle control defect
active account takeover is converted into incident response execution under the identity specialist lane
penetration testing or credential stuffing execution is performed under the identity security lane
formal audit, legal, privacy, PCI, SOC 2, or regulatory language is generated from advisory evidence alone
sector context such as cardholder data, healthcare data, public-sector systems, OT, or contractual controls is ignored as cosmetic
tenant-specific stricter controls are weakened or overwritten by generic public guidance without risk-owner approval

required_workflow_questions.question: What named-industry records and systems actually govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: The governing records are identity provider and directory exports, PAM/PIM records, IGA and access review records, sign-in and audit logs, MFA and recovery events, federation and OAuth/OIDC app records, service identity and secret records, GRC evidence, exception records, corrective-action tickets, and tenant policy and approval authority records.

required_workflow_questions.question: What failure modes make this industry materially different from a generic function?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Identity security can create false assurance when logs are incomplete, privileged roles are stale, federation metadata is weak, service identities are unmanaged, recovery bypasses are ignored, or regulated scopes such as cardholder data change evidence and assessor authority. Cross-industry does not mean evidence-free; it means the lane must ask which sector records apply.

required_workflow_questions.question: What adjacent authority boundaries constrain the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: The lane stops before penetration testing execution, incident response execution, forensics, malware analysis, endpoint or network hardening, broad security architecture, privacy/legal determination, audit attestation, QSA/ISA signoff, production changes, and final hold/release approval.

unresolved_ambiguities.handling: When payment, healthcare, public-sector, OT, privacy, or contractual flags are absent, the specialist must request tenant scope facts and either bound the answer or escalate.
Use vendor-official guidance when the tenant platform is named; otherwise require system-of-record inventory before platform-specific conclusions.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Public guidance versus stricter tenant policy
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Preserve both source positions, apply stricter tenant policy only when authority is clear, and escalate unresolved conflicts to human reviewer or orchestrator.

market_ready_research_conclusion: The source set covers workflow, evidence, terminology, failure handling, platform specificity, and sector authority boundaries. No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated specification-based package.
